    • Document version history

ESS voting pattern study Version 3

Introduction

This document presents preliminary descriptive and multivariate analysis of voter turnout at the last national elections among respondents aged 50+ using data from the European Social Survey - Round 11 (2023-24). 1

  • Section 1 presents the variables included in the analysis
  • Section 2 discusses recoding related issues
  • Section 3 includes unweighted and weighted frequency tables of unrecoded, recoded and derived socio-economic (SEV) and independent (IV) variables.
  • Section 4 documents unweighted and weighted 2-way contingency tables of SEVs and IVs by turnout at the last national elections, as well as age and sex.
  • Section 5 presents additional four way tables of voter turnout by SEVs and IVs broken down by age and sex.
  • Section 6 presents the results of four series of stepwise logistic regression models of voting at the last national election.

1. Variables included

Table 1: Original and derived variables used in the analysis
Description Name Variable label Valid responses 2
Age 2 categories AgeCat2 Recoded age, 2 category 897
Age 3 categories AgeCat3 NULL 897
Subjective financial situation hincfel Feeling about household’s income nowadays 888
Loneliness fltlnl Felt lonely, how often past week 894
Sex gndr Felt lonely, how often past week 897
Happiness happy How happy are you 894
Social life intensity sclmeet How often socially meet with friends, relatives or colleagues 896
Religious attendence rlgatnd How often attend religious services apart from special occasions 896
Trade union or similar membership mbtru Member of trade union or similar organisation 888
Whether doing house- or care work hswrk Doing last 7 days: housework, looking after children, others 897
Type of organisation working/worked for tporgwk What type of organisation work/worked for 859
Whether volunteered volunfp Volunteered for not-for-profit or charitable organisation 897
Whether voter at the last GE vote Voted last national election 889
Interest for politics polintr How interested in politics 896
Internet usage netusoft Internet use, how often 897
Marital/relationship status maritalb Legal marital status, post coded 888
Educational level eisced Highest level of education, ES - ISCED 886
Self-reported general health V1 health Subjective general health 895
Self-reported general health V2 health2 NULL 895
High blood pressure hltphhb Health problems, hampered, last 12 month: high blood pressure 897
Allergies hltphal Health problems, hampered, last 12 month: allergies 897
Back or neck pain hltphbn Health problems, hampered, last 12 month: back or neck pain 897
Muscular or joint pain in hand or arm hltphpa Health problems, hampered, last 12 month: muscular or joint pain in hand or arm 897
Muscular or joint pain in foot or leg hltphpf Health problems, hampered, last 12 month: muscular or joint pain in foot or leg 897
Stomach or digestion related hltphsd Health problems, hampered, last 12 month: stomach or digestion related 897
Skin condition related hltphsc Health problems, hampered, last 12 month: skin condition related 897
Severe headaches hltphsh Health problems, hampered, last 12 month: severe headaches 897
Diabetes hltphdi Health problems, hampered, last 12 month: diabetes 897
Number of reported health problems nrhltpb NULL 897
Recoded household type hhdtypb NULL 897
Occupation, ISCO08 1 digit isco1 NULL 837
Economic activity mnactic Main activity, last 7 days. All respondents. Post coded 893

2. Variables recoding and operationalisation notes

The original dataset includes 46,162 observations, of which 897 respondents aged 50 and over.

  1. Integrated household composition variable hhdtypb was constructed using the household grid variables rshipa*, and household size information.
  2. No information was available on the presence of grandchilddren
  3. There is no clear variable recording exclusively caring for dependents: it is either amalgamated with housework (hwrk) or with helping others more broadly: hlpfmly.
  4. Whether respondents had internet connection was approximated using frequency of internet use.
  5. Marital status was constructed in order to include informal partnership/cohabitation.
  6. There are no variables measuring difficulties/limitations with daily life activities. A variable was constructed recording the number of health conditions instead.

3. Univariates results

Results are presented as follows, under each tab below:

  1. Unweighted frequencies and proportions of the original variables, unrecoded
  2. Weighted frequencies and proportions of the recoded (ie missing/invalid values removed) variables

In the case of derived variables created for the purpose of this analysis 1. and 2. are identical.

  • Whether voter at the last GE
  • Subjective financial situation
  • Loneliness
  • Sex
  • Happiness
  • Social life intensity
  • Religious attendence
  • Trade union or similar membership
  • Whether doing house- or care work
  • Type of organisation working/worked for
  • Whether volunteered
  • Interest for politics
  • Internet usage
  • Marital/relationship status
  • Educational level
  • Self-reported general health V1
  • High blood pressure
  • Allergies
  • Back or neck pain
  • Muscular or joint pain in hand or arm
  • Muscular or joint pain in foot or leg
  • Stomach or digestion related
  • Skin condition related
  • Severe headaches
  • Diabetes
  • Economic activity
  • Self-reported general health V2
  • Number of reported health problems
  • Recoded household type
  • Occupation, ISCO08 1 digit

4. Bivariates results

This section contains for each variable used in the study, the following contingency tables:

  1. With voter turnout at the last national elections
  2. With sex
  3. With age, two and three categories

All contingency tables were computed with weighted data and include \(\chi^2\) test of independence.

4.1. Voter turnout at the last national elections

  • Subjective financial situation
  • Loneliness
  • Sex
  • Happiness
  • Social life intensity
  • Religious attendence
  • Trade union or similar membership
  • Whether doing house- or care work
  • Type of organisation working/worked for
  • Whether volunteered
  • Interest for politics
  • Internet usage
  • Marital/relationship status
  • Educational level
  • Self-reported general health V1
  • High blood pressure
  • Allergies
  • Back or neck pain
  • Muscular or joint pain in hand or arm
  • Muscular or joint pain in foot or leg
  • Stomach or digestion related
  • Skin condition related
  • Severe headaches
  • Diabetes
  • Economic activity
  • Recoded age, 2 category
  • Recoded age, 3 category
  • Self-reported general health V2
  • Number of reported health problems
  • Recoded household type
  • Occupation, ISCO08 1 digit
  • Recoded age, 2 category
  • Recoded age, 3 category

5. Further descriptives

This section presents:

  • Unweighted three way tables of age (two categories) and sex by whether voted at the last national elections. These are unweighted due to the small number of observations for a significant number of categories.

  • Plot of cross-sectional (weighted) proportions of 50+ who cast their ballot at the GE, by election year, sex and age

5.1 IVs by age, sex and whether voted at the 2019 GE

  • Subjective financial situation
  • Loneliness
  • Sex
  • Happiness
  • Social life intensity
  • Religious attendence
  • Trade union or similar membership
  • Whether doing house- or care work
  • Type of organisation working/worked for
  • Whether volunteered
  • Interest for politics
  • Internet usage
  • Marital/relationship status
  • Educational level
  • Self-reported general health V1
  • High blood pressure
  • Allergies
  • Back or neck pain
  • Muscular or joint pain in hand or arm
  • Muscular or joint pain in foot or leg
  • Stomach or digestion related
  • Skin condition related
  • Severe headaches
  • Diabetes
  • Economic activity
  • Recoded age, 2 category
  • Recoded age, 3 category
  • Self-reported general health V2
  • Number of reported health problems
  • Recoded household type
  • Occupation, ISCO08 1 digit

5.2 Voting pattern by age, sex and general election

6. Multivariate results

This section presents the results of two series of stepwise logistic regression models of voter turnout at the last national elections against:

  1. Socio-economic variables
  • Marital/relationship status
  • Household composition
  • Age
  • Education
  • Economic activity
  1. Independent variables (IVs):
  • Internet usage;
  • Whether feels lonely;
  • Self-rated general health;
  • Number of health conditions
  • Financial situation;
  • Happiness;
  • Whether member of union or group;
  • Whether a carer
  • Religious attendance
  • Intensity of social life
  • Interest in politics
  • Type of organisation worked for
  • Involvement in volunteering

All models were fitted using weights.

  • 6.1 Socio-economic variables & whether voted at previous GE
  • 6.2 SEV + IVS
  • 6.3 SEV + IVS + interactions
  • 6.4 SEV & interaction

Stepwise models

Coefficient plot of Models 6

Document version history

Version 3 - UK-only analysis on a separate page

Version 2:

  • Amended reference categories for marital status and education in regression tables to match those fromn ELSA
  • Changed health problems mention to impairing health problems mentioned
  • Added two regression tables with the interaction between Age 3 categories and sex included

Initial version

To do:

  1. Check if control for simplified country blocks hass an impact;
  2. Improve landing page.

Footnotes

  1. Israel, the Russian Federation and Turkey were not included in this analysis.↩︎

  2. Respondents aged 50 and over]↩︎
